params.text:
Working on your own

A lone worker is anyone who carries out their job without close or direct supervision, and without a colleague immediately on hand to help. In adult social care this is an everyday reality. You are a lone worker when you drive between visits, let yourself into someone's home, deliver personal care behind a closed front door, or cover a night shift while the rest of the team sleeps.

Lone working is not unusual and it is not, by itself, dangerous. Millions of people do it safely every day. What matters is that the risks are understood, assessed and controlled so that you are no more at risk than a colleague working alongside others.

  • Domiciliary and community staff visiting people in their own homes, often back to back across a wide area.
  • Night staff in a care home or supported living setting, where fewer people are on shift.
  • Anyone travelling alone between calls, or working in a part of a building away from others.

params.question: There is a specific law that bans care staff from working alone.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. There is no law banning lone working. What the law does require is that the risks of working alone are assessed and properly controlled.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. No law prohibits lone working. Instead, the law requires your employer to assess and control the risks so you can work alone safely.
